english: 'Ab' (off) + 'Schluss' (closing).\nA chapter ending, a new one beginning.
The same word for finishing school (Schulabschluss) and finishing a story. Tonight is an Abschluss: the closing of one chapter, the beginning of the next.
'Leben' (life) + 'Abschnitt' (section).\nA distinct period in someone's life.
Moving to Berlin was the start of a new Lebensabschnitt. This dinner marks where one ends and the next begins.
'Meile' (mile) + 'Stein' (stone).\nA marker of distance traveled.
Same metaphor as English: a stone marking distance. The B2 exam. The apartment. The friends. All Meilensteine on a journey that started at an airport.
'Vor' (before) + 'Freude' (joy).\nThe happiness you feel BEFORE the event.
A famously untranslatable German word. 'Vorfreude ist die schoenste Freude' (Anticipation is the sweetest joy). The happiness of knowing something wonderful is coming.
'Willkommen' (welcome) + 'Gruss' (greeting).\nWhat you say at the door when guests arrive.
Tonight, Verumius gives the Willkommensgruss. He is the host. Two years ago he could barely say 'Hallo.'
'Dankbar' (grateful) + '-keit' suffix.\nThe abstract feeling of being grateful.
The '-keit' suffix turns adjectives into abstract nouns. The feeling that every person at this table has given you something you cannot repay.
'Herzlich' (heartfelt) + '-keit' suffix.\nFrom 'Herz' (heart). Warmth from the heart.
From 'Herz' (heart). Warmth that comes from the heart. What Verumius wants his dinner to radiate tonight.
Reflexive verb. With 'auf' = future.\nWith 'über' = present or past.
Two prepositions, two timeframes. 'Sich freuen auf' (looking forward to something not yet here). 'Sich freuen über' (happy about something already here). The direction of joy.
From Middle High German 'stunen.'\nStronger than 'überrascht sein.'
Staunen implies wonder, not just surprise. When you staunen, you stand in awe. Stronger than being surprised. The feeling of witnessing something that moves you.
'Be-' + 'wundern' (to wonder).\nNot separable. Active admiration.
The 'be-' prefix makes 'wonder' into active admiration directed at someone. Not just passively wondering but actively looking up to.
'Herz' (heart) + '-lich' suffix.\nNot formal, not casual: heartfelt.
The adjective that describes how Germans greet people they genuinely care about. 'Herzlich willkommen!' is the warmest welcome there is.
'Feier' (celebration) + '-lich' suffix.\nCan mean joyful OR serious.
Feierlich holds both moods: joyful celebration and serious ceremony. A wedding is feierlich. A graduation is feierlich. This dinner is feierlich.
'Dank' (thanks) + '-bar' suffix.\nAble to give thanks.
The '-bar' suffix means 'able to.' 'Dankbar' = able to give thanks. 'Dankbar' is what you are. 'Dankbarkeit' is what you feel.
'Auf' (up) + 'richtig' (right/straight).\nLiterally 'upright.' Honesty made physical.
An aufrichtiger Mensch stands straight, hides nothing. 'Aufrichtigen Dank' (sincere thanks). Honesty and integrity captured in a posture.
'Gelassen' (calm) + '-heit' suffix.\nThe art of letting go. From 'lassen' (to let).
The quality Germans most admire in a crisis: calm, unshaken, steady. Gelassenheit is letting things be. The opposite of panic.
'Leben' (life) + 'Freude' (joy).\nNot just being happy, but alive to joy itself.
Another untranslatable gem. Not just being happy, but being alive to the joy of existence itself. A person with Lebensfreude radiates it.
'Leiden' (to suffer) + '-schaft' suffix.\nWhat you love so much you suffer for it.
Passion literally contains suffering in German. 'Leiden' = to suffer. The deepest dedication: what you love so much that you suffer for it.
'Selbst' (self) + 'Vertrauen' (trust).\nTrust in yourself. Quiet certainty.
Not arrogance but the quiet certainty that you can handle what comes. What Verumius has built over two years in Berlin.
'Wohl' (well) + 'Befinden' (state/condition).\nHow you are doing in body and mind.
'Wie ist Ihr Wohlbefinden?' is what doctors ask. The holistic state of being well: body, mind, and spirit together.
'Zu' (toward) + 'Versicht' (foresight).\nLooking forward with grounded trust.
Not blind optimism but grounded confidence. 'Mit Zuversicht in die Zukunft' (with confidence into the future). Trust based on experience.
From Middle High German 'gelucke.'\nOne word for both happiness AND luck.
German uses one word for both happiness and luck. 'Glück haben' (to be lucky) and 'glücklich sein' (to be happy). Fortune and joy share a name.
Rest, quiet, peace. Inner calm or outer silence.\n'Ruhe bitte!' (Quiet please!)
'In Ruhe essen' (to eat in peace). 'Die Ruhe vor dem Sturm' (the calm before the storm). Inner peace, outer silence, or both at once.
From Old High German 'stolz' (proud/stately).\nEarned pride, not arrogance.
'Stolz sein auf' (to be proud of). The quiet swelling when you have earned something. Not arrogance: earned pride. What Verumius feels tonight.
'Auf' (up/open) + 'blühen' (to bloom).\nSeparable. The image of a flower opening.
A flower opening to the sun. What happens to a person who finds their place. Separable: 'Er blüht auf.' Past: 'Er ist aufgeblüht.'
Strong verb: geniessen/genoss/genossen.\nConsciously appreciating, not just consuming.
From Old High German 'niozan' (to enjoy/use). Not just consuming but consciously appreciating. What tonight's dinner is for.
From 'Schatz' (treasure).\nWhen you treat something as precious.
When you treat something as a Schatz (treasure), you schätzen it. Appreciation and valuation in one word. Treasuring what matters.
Past participle of 'lassen' (to let).\nThe person who has learned to let go.
The person who has learned to let go of what they cannot control. Gelassenheit (serenity) comes from being gelassen. Calm through release.
'Aus' (out) + 'geglichen' (equalized).\nFrom 'ausgleichen' (to balance out).
Someone whose inner scales are level. Not pulled to extremes. An ausgeglichener Mensch stays steady through storms.
Same meaning, different register.\n'Beginnen' = formal. 'Anfangen' = casual.
Register pair. 'Beginnen' sits in official language: announcements, invitations, speeches. 'Anfangen' sits at the kitchen table. Same meaning, different world.
'Erwerben' implies effort or merit.\n'Kaufen' is a simple transaction.
'Kenntnisse erwerben' (to acquire knowledge) vs. 'Brot kaufen' (to buy bread). Erwerben carries weight and effort. Kaufen is a transaction.
'Sich äußern' = deliberate statement.\n'Sagen' = everyday conversation.
One is a formal statement to an audience. The other is turning to a friend. Yilmaz äußert sich. Lukas sagt.
'Verweilen' = literary, poetic.\n'Bleiben' = daily life.
'Verweile doch, du bist so schoen' is from Goethe's Faust. Poetry vs. daily life. 'Bleibst du noch?' is what friends ask at midnight.
'Gestatten' = courtly formality.\n'Erlauben' = standard permission.
Yilmaz says 'Gestatten.' Lukas says 'Darf ich.' Both mean 'May I?' but one belongs to formal courtesy, the other to everyday speech.
'Beabsichtigen' = stated intention.\n'Wollen' = direct desire.
Intention vs. desire. 'Beabsichtigen' has four syllables and the weight of a formal declaration. 'Wollen' has two syllables and the force of pure will.
'Entgegnen' = literary, weighty reply.\n'Antworten' = standard answering.
'Entgegnen' has more weight. It implies a considered reply, often with a counterpoint. Narrative language. 'Antworten' is neutral reporting.
'Umarmen' (to embrace) + '-ung' suffix.\nArms around someone.
From 'um' (around) + 'Arm' (arm). Putting your arms around someone. What happens at this doorway tonight with every arriving guest.
'Erfahrung' (experience) + 'Schatz' (treasure).\nA treasure of lived experience.
What every person at this table brings: a treasure of lived experience. Hildi's decades. Yilmaz's languages. Heinrich's bread. All Schaetze.
'Erinnerung' (memory) + 'Stück' (piece).\nA physical piece of a memory.
Every object in Verumius's apartment is an Erinnerungsstück: the corkboard, the essay, the photos, the ticket stubs. Memory made tangible.
'Um' (around) + 'Arme' (arms).\nInseparable prefix. Not 'um...armen.'
Not separable (inseparable um-). The physical act of putting arms around another person. What this dinner is, in one verb.
Reflexive. 'Sich erinnern an' + accusative.\nLiterally: to make something inner again.
'Er-' + 'innern' (from 'inner'). To make something inner again. Bringing the past back inside you. Memory as an inward journey.
'Liebe' (love) + 'voll' (full).\nFull of love.
What Heinrich's sourdough gift is. What Hildi's casserole is. What this whole evening is. Full of love.
From 'innen' (inside/inner).\nMore intense than 'herzlich.'
Something that comes from deep inside. Reserved for the closest bonds. More intense than 'herzlich.' An innige Umarmung is the kind that makes you close your eyes.
'Sinne' (senses/mind) + 'Wandel' (change).\nA fundamental shift in perspective.
When someone shifts their fundamental perspective. Verumius had a Sinneswandel: Berlin went from foreign to home. From 'ich muss hier weg' to 'ich will hier bleiben.'
'Leben' (life) + 'Motto' (motto).\nWhat guides your decisions.
Every character at this table has one, spoken or unspoken. Hildi's: 'Man macht das eben.' Heinrich's: 'Gutes Brot braucht Zeit.' Yilmaz's: 'Sprache ist Heimat.'
From 'reif' (ripe/mature).\nApplied to fruit and people alike.
'Charakterliche Reife' (emotional maturity). What Verumius has gained. Not just language skills but understanding. The ripening that only time and experience bring.
From 'besinnen' (to reflect/recollect).\n'Zur Besinnung kommen' = to come to one's senses.
The act of stopping and thinking deeply. 'Zur Besinnung kommen' means both 'to regain consciousness' and 'to come to your senses.' Physical and mental awakening.
'Blick' (gaze) + 'Winkel' (angle).\nThe angle from which you see the world.
Literally 'the angle of your gaze.' Each person at this table sees the world from a different Blickwinkel. That is what makes the dinner rich.
'Mit' (with) + 'Gefühl' (feeling).\nFeeling WITH someone, not just FOR them.
Not sympathy (feeling FOR someone) but empathy (feeling WITH them). The deeper connection. 'Mit' makes all the difference: you stand beside, not above.
Reflexive. Strong: besinnen/besann/besonnen.\nTo turn your mind inward.
'Be-' + 'sinnen' (to think/ponder). To turn your mind inward. Also: 'sich eines Besseren besinnen' (to think better of it). Reflection that changes action.
'Nach' (about) + 'denken' (to think).\nSeparable. Deeper than 'denken' alone.
Thinking ABOUT something carefully. 'Denken' is just thinking. 'Nachdenken' is deliberate contemplation. The 'nach' adds depth and direction.
'Inne' (within) + 'halten' (to stop).\nSeparable. Stillness plus reflection.
The physical act of stopping combined with inner reflection. A moment of stillness in the middle of motion. What Verumius does when he holds Hildi's place card.
'Los' (loose/released) + 'lassen' (to let).\nSeparable. The hardest life skill.
Letting go of control, fear, the past. What Gelassenheit requires. The separable prefix 'los' means 'loose, free.' 'Lass los!' = Let go!
Past participle of 'besinnen.'\nSomeone who acts from reflection, not impulse.
Someone who has reflected and acts from that reflection. Not impulsive. Considered. The adjective form of wisdom earned through 'sich besinnen.'
'Nachdenken' + '-lich' suffix.\nThe adjective of deep thinking.
A nachdenklicher Blick (thoughtful gaze) is what philosophers and old friends share over wine. The visible sign of inner contemplation.
'Mut' (courage) + '-ig' suffix.\nCourage in action.
